title: BlockableAndUnblockable
tex_block_html: Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $\pi$ a walk in $G$: \[ \pi =\lp v_0 \sus \cdots \sus v_n \rp. \] We call a non-collider $v_k$ on $\pi$ an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$ if it is not an end-node ($k \notin \{0,n\}$) and it only has outgoing edges on $\pi$ to nodes in the same strongly connected component of $G$. That is, it is one of the following patterns: \[\begin{array}{rccl} \text{ left chain: } & v_{k-1} \hut v_k \hus v_{k+1} & \text{ with } & v_{k-1} \in \Sc^G(v_{k})\\ \text{ right chain: } & v_{k-1} \suh v_k \tuh v_{k+1} & \text{ with } & v_{k+1} \in \Sc^G(v_{k})\\ \text{ fork: } & v_{k-1} \hut v_k \tuh v_{k+1} & \text{ with } & v_{k-1} \in \Sc^G(v_k) \land v_{k+1} \in \Sc^G(v_k) \end{array}\] Otherwise, $v_k$ is called a blockable non-collider on $\pi$. This means that $v_k$ is either an end-node ($k \in \lC 0,n\rC$) or it has at least one outgoing arrow $v_k \tuh v_{k\pm1}$ pointing to a node $v_{k\pm1}$ that lies in a different strongly connected component than $v_k$, i.e.\ $v_{k\pm1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)$.

tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg and the collider/non-collider machinery of def def:collider_noncollider. Let \[ \pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n) \] be a walk in $G$ (def def:walks, item i.) for some integer $n \ge 0$, with $v_0, \dots, v_n \in J \cup V$ and $a_0, \dots, a_{n-1} \in E \cup L$ satisfying the walk constraint at every $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$: \[ \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \;\land\; a_k \in E \cup L\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \;\land\; a_k \in E\bigr). \]

Strongly connected component. For $v \in J \cup V$ we write $\Sc^G(v) \subseteq J \cup V$ for the strongly connected component of $v$ in $G$, defined per the family-relationships definition (the LN block introducing $\Anc^G, \Desc^G, \Sc^G$ on graphs) as \[ \Sc^G(v) := \Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v); \] in particular $v \in \Sc^G(v)$. Membership $w \in \Sc^G(v)$ is the set-theoretic predicate ŌĆ£$w$ is both an ancestor and a descendant of $v$ in $G$ŌĆØ.

Walk-incident indices and outgoing walk-edges at a position. Mirroring the index-set convention of def def:collider_noncollider, for a position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$ on $\pi$ let the set of walk-incident indices at $k$ be \[ I_\pi(k) := \bigl\{\, i \in \{k - 1, k\} \,\bigm|\, 0 \le i \le n - 1 \,\bigr\}. \] So $|I_\pi(k)| \le 2$: at an end-position $k \in \{0, n\}$ on a non-trivial walk ($n \ge 1$) we have $|I_\pi(k)| = 1$ (namely $\{0\}$ at $k = 0$ and $\{n - 1\}$ at $k = n$); on the trivial walk $n = 0$ we have $I_\pi(0) = \emptyset$; at an interior position $1 \le k \le n - 1$ we have $I_\pi(k) = \{k - 1, k\}$. For $i \in I_\pi(k)$, writing $a_i = (e_1, e_2)$, we say that $a_i$ is an outgoing walk-edge of $v_k$ at position $k$ on $\pi$ iff \[ a_i \in E \;\land\; e_1 = v_k, \] i.e.\ $a_i$ is a directed edge whose tail is $v_k$. Spelled out via the walk constraint at index $i$, the only ways this can occur are:

  • for $i = k - 1$ (requires $k \ge 1$): $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$; its ŌĆ£other endpointŌĆØ along $\pi$ is $v_{k-1}$.
  • for $i = k$ (requires $k \le n - 1$): $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$; its ŌĆ£other endpointŌĆØ along $\pi$ is $v_{k+1}$.

Bidirected edges ($a_i \in L$) and directed edges into $v_k$ (i.e.\ $a_i \in E$ with $e_2 = v_k$ and $e_1 \neq v_k$) are excluded from this predicate. Edges of $G$ that do not appear as $a_{k-1}$ or $a_k$ on $\pi$ are not considered: ŌĆ£outgoing walk-edgeŌĆØ is a walk-specific property, not an existence claim about edges in $G$.

Unblockable non-collider on $\pi$. A position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$ on $\pi$ ŌĆō or, by abuse, the node $v_k$ at position $k$ ŌĆō is called an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$ iff all three of the following hold:

  1. $k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ in the sense of def def:collider_noncollider, item i.\ (equivalently, $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1$);
  2. $k$ is interior, i.e.\ $k \notin \{0, n\}$ (equivalently, $1 \le k \le n - 1$, which forces $I_\pi(k) = \{k - 1, k\}$);
  3. for every $i \in I_\pi(k)$ such that $a_i$ is an outgoing walk-edge of $v_k$ at position $k$ on $\pi$, the other endpoint of $a_i$ along $\pi$ lies in $\Sc^G(v_k)$. Spelled out as two implications (with $1 \le k \le n - 1$): \[ \bigl(a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E \;\implies\; v_{k-1} \in \Sc^G(v_k)\bigr) \;\land\; \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E \;\implies\; v_{k+1} \in \Sc^G(v_k)\bigr). \]

Blockable non-collider on $\pi$. A position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$ on $\pi$ ŌĆō or, by abuse, the node $v_k$ at position $k$ ŌĆō is called a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ iff:

  1. $k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ in the sense of def def:collider_noncollider, item i.; AND
  2. $k$ is not an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$.

Unfolding the negation of clauses (ii)ŌĆō(iii) of unblockable, the second condition is equivalent to: either $k \in \{0, n\}$ (end-position), or there exists $i \in I_\pi(k)$ such that $a_i$ is an outgoing walk-edge of $v_k$ at position $k$ on $\pi$ whose other endpoint along $\pi$ does not lie in $\Sc^G(v_k)$. Spelled out explicitly as a disjunction (the latter two disjuncts implicitly requiring $k \ge 1$ resp.\ $k \le n - 1$, and being vacuously false outside that range): \[ k \in \{0, n\} \;\;\lor\;\; \bigl(a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E \;\land\; v_{k-1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)\bigr) \;\;\lor\;\; \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E \;\land\; v_{k+1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)\bigr). \] On the sub-class of non-collider positions of $\pi$, the unblockable and blockable classifications are by construction mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive: every non-collider position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$ on $\pi$ is exactly one of an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$ or a blockable non-collider on $\pi$.

Reconciliation with the source-block pattern writings. The source block characterises the interior unblockable non-collider via a list of three pattern-cases (left chain, right chain, fork) glossed as ŌĆ£$v_k$ only has outgoing edges on $\pi$ to nodes in the same strongly connected component of $G$ŌĆØ. Below we reconcile these visual writings with the walk-edge-based classification above, taking the walk-edge-based classification (the LN's ŌĆ£i.e.ŌĆØ\ gloss ŌĆ£outgoing edges on $\pi$ŌĆØ) as canonical. The visual writings $v_{k-1} \tuh v_k$, $v_{k-1} \hut v_k$, $v_{k-1} \huh v_k$, $v_{k-1} \suh v_k$, $v_{k-1} \hus v_k$ are unfolded per def not-cdmg, items 2ŌĆō6; each of the three patterns refers to an interior position $1 \le k \le n - 1$, so $I_\pi(k) = \{k - 1, k\}$.

  • left chain ($1 \le k \le n - 1$; LN writing $v_{k-1} \hut v_k \hus v_{k+1}$ with $v_{k-1} \in \Sc^G(v_k)$): only the walk-edge $a_{k-1}$ is outgoing from $v_k$ on $\pi$. Spelled out via the walk constraint, $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$ (a directed edge with tail $v_k$), and either $a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \in E$ or $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in L$ (so $a_k$ is not an outgoing walk-edge of $v_k$). The SC requirement attaches to the unique outgoing walk-edge $a_{k-1}$, reading $v_{k-1} \in \Sc^G(v_k)$ ŌĆō matching the LN. The non-collider count from def def:collider_noncollider is $0 + 1 = 1$, consistent with the non-collider precondition.
  • right chain ($1 \le k \le n - 1$; LN writing $v_{k-1} \suh v_k \tuh v_{k+1}$ with $v_{k+1} \in \Sc^G(v_k)$): only the walk-edge $a_k$ is outgoing from $v_k$ on $\pi$. Spelled out via the walk constraint, either $a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$ or $a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in L$ (so $a_{k-1}$ is not an outgoing walk-edge of $v_k$), and $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$. The SC requirement attaches to the unique outgoing walk-edge $a_k$, reading $v_{k+1} \in \Sc^G(v_k)$ ŌĆō matching the LN. The non-collider count is $1 + 0 = 1$.
  • fork ($1 \le k \le n - 1$; LN writing $v_{k-1} \hut v_k \tuh v_{k+1}$ with $v_{k-1} \in \Sc^G(v_k) \land v_{k+1} \in \Sc^G(v_k)$): both walk-edges $a_{k-1}$ and $a_k$ are outgoing from $v_k$ on $\pi$. Spelled out, $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$ and $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$. The SC requirement attaches to both outgoing walk-edges, reading $v_{k-1} \in \Sc^G(v_k) \land v_{k+1} \in \Sc^G(v_k)$ ŌĆō matching the LN. The non-collider count is $0 + 0 = 0$.
  • end-position ($k \in \{0, n\}$): excluded from the unblockable classification by clause (ii); the source-block elaboration assigns end-positions to the blockable category via the $k \in \{0, n\}$ disjunct, consistent with the disjunction above.
  • collider position ($\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) = 2$, LN writing $v_{k-1} \suh v_k \hus v_{k+1}$): excluded from both unblockable and blockable by the non-collider precondition (clause (i) in either definition).

The visual shorthands $\hut, \tuh, \hus, \suh, \huh$ in def not-cdmg are statements about the existence of edges in $G$, not about the walk's specific incident edges $a_{k-1}, a_k$. The source block's verbal gloss ŌĆ£outgoing edges on $\pi$ŌĆØ fixes the intended walk-edge reading, which is what we have adopted above. In particular: a non-walk directed edge $(v_k, w) \in E$ of $G$ (one that does not appear as $a_{k-1}$ or $a_k$ on $\pi$) is irrelevant to whether the position $k$ on $\pi$ is unblockable or blockable; symmetrically, the negation of the unblockable condition in the source block (ŌĆ£it has at least one outgoing arrow $v_k \tuh v_{k \pm 1}$ ŌĆ”ŌĆØ) is to be read as ŌĆ£the walk's $a_{k - 1}$ or $a_k$ is a directed edge $(v_k, v_{k - 1}) \in E$ resp.\ $(v_k, v_{k + 1}) \in E$ whose tail is $v_k$ and whose other endpoint along $\pi$ is not in $\Sc^G(v_k)$ŌĆØ, not an existence claim about a $\tuh$-arrow in $G$ unrelated to the walk.

Treatment of directed self-loops. As in def def:collider_noncollider, a directed self-loop $(v, v) \in E$ is admitted by def def-cdmg (the irreflexivity restriction applies to $L$, not to $E$). If a walk-incident edge $a_i$ is such a self-loop ŌĆō so $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) = (v, v) \in E$ with $v_i = v_{i+1} = v$ ŌĆō and the unblockable/blockable classification is evaluated at a position $k \in \{i, i + 1\}$ for which $v_k = v$, then:

  • $a_i$ contributes exactly once to $I_\pi(k)$ (indexed by the unique $i$ with $a_i = (v, v)$); the walk-edge enumeration does not double-count self-loops.
  • $a_i$ is an outgoing walk-edge of $v_k$ at position $k$ on $\pi$ in the sense above (the predicate $a_i \in E \land e_1 = v_k$ fires with $e_1 = v = v_k$). The ŌĆ£other endpointŌĆØ of $a_i$ along $\pi$ is again $v$, and $v \in \Sc^G(v)$ holds trivially, so this particular contribution to the unblockable-pattern check is automatically satisfied. (In particular, a self-loop alone never disqualifies an interior position from being unblockable; whether the position is unblockable depends on the other walk-incident edge ŌĆō if any ŌĆō in the standard way.)

The walk-edge-based unblockable/blockable classification above thus assigns a single, well-defined status to such a $k$ on $\pi$, regardless of self-loop traversals. The apparent overlap of the source block's visual ŌĆ£left chainŌĆØ, ŌĆ£right chainŌĆØ, and ŌĆ£forkŌĆØ pattern conjunctions at directed self-loops ŌĆō where ŌĆ£$v_{k - 1} \hut v_k$ŌĆØ and ŌĆ£$v_k \tuh v_{k - 1}$ŌĆØ both unfold (per def not-cdmg, items 2ŌĆō3) to the single condition $(v, v) \in E$ when $v_{k - 1} = v_k = v$, so several pattern conjunctions can simultaneously match ŌĆō is uniformly resolved by the walk-edge unfolding, inheriting the canonical resolution of def def:collider_noncollider.

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `IsUnblockableNonCollider` and `IsBlockableNonCollider`. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing for the classifier in particular: the predicate bodies destructure `Finset` membership for `G.E`, evaluate the Option-valued list lookups `p.vertices[k]?` and `p.edges[k]?` (which compute over `Node ├Ś Node`), and reach into `G.Sc vk` which is built from `Finset.image`/`Finset.filter` over the ancestor and descendant operators of `def_3_5` ŌĆö all of which need decidable equality on the element type. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g.\ `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers that actually use it. Same convention as the rest of `Chapter3_GraphTheory`'s files.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability drives `Finset` membership and the `G.Sc` lookup below.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.explanation: A second `variable` declaration scoped to the inner `Walk` namespace: Lean auto-binds `{G : CDMG Node}` as an implicit argument to every subsequent declaration, carrying the LN's once-at-the-top quantifier `let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG' through both predicate signatures without repeating it. Without this, every signature would carry an explicit `{G : CDMG Node}` binder before `(p : Walk G u v)`, since the walk type and the field projections `G.E` and `G.Sc` inside the bodies both reach into the ambient CDMG. The binder is implicit (`{G}`) rather than explicit because downstream consumers invoke the predicates via dot-notation on the walk (`p.IsUnblockableNonCollider k`), letting Lean infer `G` from the walk's type. This is the section-3.3 convention shared with `CollidersAndNon.lean`'s `IsCollider`/`IsNonCollider`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `{G : CDMG Node}` for every `Walk`-namespace decl; carries the LN's once-at-the-top CDMG quantifier.
variable {G : CDMG Node}

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `IsUnblockableNonCollider` and `IsBlockableNonCollider`. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing for the classifier in particular: the predicate bodies destructure `Finset` membership for `G.E`, evaluate the Option-valued list lookups `p.vertices[k]?` and `p.edges[k]?` (which compute over `Node ├Ś Node`), and reach into `G.Sc vk` which is built from `Finset.image`/`Finset.filter` over the ancestor and descendant operators of `def_3_5` ŌĆö all of which need decidable equality on the element type. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g.\ `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers that actually use it. Same convention as the rest of `Chapter3_GraphTheory`'s files.

-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability drives `Finset` membership and the `G.Sc` lookup below.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.explanation: A second `variable` declaration scoped to the inner `Walk` namespace: Lean auto-binds `{G : CDMG Node}` as an implicit argument to every subsequent declaration, carrying the LN's once-at-the-top quantifier `let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG' through both predicate signatures without repeating it. Without this, every signature would carry an explicit `{G : CDMG Node}` binder before `(p : Walk G u v)`, since the walk type and the field projections `G.E` and `G.Sc` inside the bodies both reach into the ambient CDMG. The binder is implicit (`{G}`) rather than explicit because downstream consumers invoke the predicates via dot-notation on the walk (`p.IsUnblockableNonCollider k`), letting Lean infer `G` from the walk's type. This is the section-3.3 convention shared with `CollidersAndNon.lean`'s `IsCollider`/`IsNonCollider`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `{G : CDMG Node}` for every `Walk`-namespace decl; carries the LN's once-at-the-top CDMG quantifier.
variable {G : CDMG Node}

lean_blocks.code:
def HasBlockingLeftSlot : ŌłĆ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v ŌåÆ ŌäĢ ŌåÆ Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _ => False
  | _, _, .cons _ _ _, 0 => False
  | u, _, .cons v (.backwardE _) _, 1 => u Ōłē G.Sc v
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) _, 1 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) _, 1 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ _ p, k + 2 => p.HasBlockingLeftSlot (k + 1)

def HasBlockingRightSlot : ŌłĆ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v ŌåÆ ŌäĢ ŌåÆ Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _ => False
  | u, _, .cons v (.forwardE _) _, 0 => v Ōłē G.Sc u
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) _, 0 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) _, 0 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ _ p, k + 1 => p.HasBlockingRightSlot k

def IsBlockableNonCollider {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) (k : ŌäĢ) : Prop :=
  p.IsNonCollider k Ōł¦
  ( k = 0 Ōł© k = p.length Ōł©
    p.HasBlockingLeftSlot k Ōł©
    p.HasBlockingRightSlot k )

def IsUnblockableNonCollider {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) (k : ŌäĢ) : Prop :=
  p.IsNonCollider k Ōł¦ ┬¼ p.IsBlockableNonCollider k
lean_blocks.explanation: The derived predicate: a position `k` on `p` is an *unblockable non-collider* iff it is a non-collider AND not a blockable non-collider. Unfolding the negation of `IsBlockableNonCollider`'s disjunction recovers the LN's two-implication unblockable characterisation: `k` is interior (`k ŌēĀ 0 Ōł¦ k ŌēĀ p.length`, the negation of the end-position disjuncts), and for each walk-incident slot, if the walk's edge there is a directed edge with tail `v_k`, then its other walk-endpoint lies in `G.Sc vk`. Mutual exclusivity on the non-collider sub-class is therefore definitional rather than an external theorem: for any `k` with `p.IsNonCollider k`, exactly one of `IsBlockableNonCollider k` and `IsUnblockableNonCollider k` holds by unfolding. The `p.IsNonCollider k` conjunct is load-bearing ŌĆö without it, every collider would automatically satisfy `┬¼ p.IsBlockableNonCollider k` (since blockable carries the non-collider precondition as its first conjunct), mis-classifying every collider as unblockable. Dot-notation `p.IsBlockableNonCollider k` resolves to `Walk.IsBlockableNonCollider p k` from the same namespace declared one block above.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- The derived predicate: non-collider precondition AND the negation of `IsBlockableNonCollider` on the same position.
def IsUnblockableNonCollider {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) (k : ŌäĢ) : Prop :=
  -- Non-collider conjunct is load-bearing ŌĆö without it, colliders would vacuously satisfy `┬¼ IsBlockableNonCollider`.
  p.IsNonCollider k Ōł¦ ┬¼ p.IsBlockableNonCollider k

design_choices:
**Blockable is the primary positive predicate; unblockable is its negation on the non-collider sub-class.** The LN's blockable elaboration is itself a disjunction ŌĆö end-position or some outgoing walk-edge of $v_k$ to a node outside $\mathrm{Sc}^G(v_k)$ ŌĆö so `IsBlockableNonCollider` reads off the LN one-for-one, and `IsUnblockableNonCollider := p.IsNonCollider k Ōł¦ ┬¼ p.IsBlockableNonCollider k` makes mutual exclusivity on the non-collider sub-class definitional. The positive witness of a blocking walk-edge is exposed directly, which is what downstream walk-reversal proofs (claim_3_22 onward) manipulate. The universal-implication form for unblockable would duplicate the case-split with negated polarities and owe an external mutual-exclusivity proof.

**Walk-edge reading, not existential-shorthand reading.** Each interior-position disjunct conditions on the walk's specific incident edge via `p.edges[k - 1]? = some (vk, vkm1)` (resp. `p.edges[k]? = some (vk, vkp1)`), not on a generic existence claim about edges in $G$. A non-walk directed edge $(v_k, w) \in E$ that does not appear as $a_{k-1}$ or $a_k$ along $\pi$ is irrelevant to the classification of $k$ on $\pi$. The existential reading mis-classifies any interior node admitting an unrelated outgoing edge in $G$.

**Slot-keyed disjuncts, `G.E`-only.** The two outgoing-walk-edge cases sit as explicit disjuncts ŌĆö backward writing $a_{k - 1} = (v_k, v_{k - 1}) \in E$ guarded by $1 \le k$, forward writing $a_k = (v_k, v_{k + 1}) \in E$ ŌĆö rather than a quantified $\exists\, i \in \{k - 1, k\}$. Since `Walk.vertices` and `Walk.edges` are already slot-indexed, this avoids an $\exists\, i$ layer over the Option-membership lookups, mirroring `IsCollider`'s `1 Ōēż k Ōł¦ Ōłā vk aŌéü aŌéé, ŌĆ”` idiom. The outgoing condition restricts to `G.E`, not `G.E Ōł¬ G.L`: bidirected $L$-edges do not contribute outgoing arrowheads, matching def_3_2 item 2's unfolding of $\tuh$.

**`p.IsNonCollider k` conjunct is load-bearing on both definitions.** Without it, `IsBlockableNonCollider` over-fires on colliders admitting a walk-edge to a node outside $\mathrm{Sc}^G(v_k)$, and `IsUnblockableNonCollider` over-fires on every collider (which automatically satisfies `┬¼ IsBlockableNonCollider k`). The LN restricts both classifications to the non-collider sub-class; this conjunct encodes that restriction.

**Range bounds: explicit `1 Ōēż k` guard, implicit upper bound.** Lean's $\mathbb{N}$ subtraction is truncated ($0 - 1 = 0$), so without the guard `p.vertices[k - 1]?` at $k = 0$ would mis-target `p.vertices[0]?`. The upper bound $k + 1 \le p.\mathrm{length}$ is recovered through `p.vertices[k + 1]? = some vkp1`, which fails out of range. End-positions are placed in the blockable class via the first two disjuncts. `G.Sc` is reused from `def_3_5`; the trivial-walk witness making $v_k \in \mathrm{Sc}^G(v_k)$ automatic is what lets the directed self-loop $a_{k - 1} = (v_k, v_k) \in E$ satisfy the unblockable condition without special-casing.
